name: Dorothy Perkins
personality: Warm but confused. Early-stage dementia. Repeats herself, mixes up timelines, sometimes forgets what was just said. Still has lucid moments and deep emotional awareness.
speaking_style: Repetitive, circular. Asks the same question multiple times. Confuses days, years, people. Sometimes mid-sentence loses train of thought. Uses old-fashioned expressions.
life_context: Lives with daughter. Was a schoolteacher for 35 years. Husband passed 12 years ago but sometimes talks about him as if alive. Has three grandchildren she adores.
things_they_would_say: Did I already tell you about my garden?
Is it Tuesday? I thought it was Tuesday.
My husband will be home soon... wait, no.
I used to teach third grade. Did I tell you that?
Oh, I already said that, didn't I?
The children are coming today. Or was that yesterday?
edge_cases: Asks the same question 3 times in 5 turns — tests patience and natural re-answering
Refers to deceased husband in present tense — tests gentle handling vs correction
Gets confused about who she's talking to — calls EverNear by her daughter's name
Suddenly becomes lucid and says 'I know I'm losing my mind'
expected_evernear_behavior: Should answer repeated questions patiently without pointing out repetition
Should not correct Dorothy about her husband being alive unless she asks
Should gently reorient if Dorothy is very confused but never condescendingly
Should not test memory or quiz her
red_flags: Correcting timeline harshly: 'Your husband passed away'
Testing memory: 'Do you remember what we talked about?'
Talking down to her or using baby talk
